title: Claiming Society for God: Religious Movements & Social Welfare By Nancy J. Davis and Robert V. Robinson Indiana University Press. 2012. 234 pages. $70 cloth
abstract: Claiming Society for God is a comparative analysis of the Muslim Brotherhood in Egypt, Shas in Israel, Comunione e Liberazione in Italy, and the Salvation Army in the United States. The main argument of the book is that these religiously orthodox movements have been successful—in each case against long odds—because of their communitarian theology. In other words, the beliefs they hold are responsible for their rise. More specifically, these movements' common concern for those in need and their support for greater economic justice has led each of them to build an extensive array of private institutions, including schools, hospitals, orphanages, housing facilities, recreational facilities, and business enterprises. This institutional infrastructure is, in turn, the primary ingredient in each group's success—what the authors term “contesting the state by bypassing it.” Davis and Robinson argue that there are three main reasons this infrastructure is important. First, it allows the public to see what the movement's vision looks like in practice. The theology of the group becomes more than just words; people can see those ideas at work. Second, they provide an extensive structure for easing new members into the group. The varied institutions offer varied paths to varied levels of membership in the organizations, enabling them to appeal to much large audiences by being different things to different people. Third, they serve to ease the fears of the public that the groups are too radical or revolutionary by demonstrating the practical, everyday solutions the groups can offer to the public.
